perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall come into force on the date both parties have executed this Agreement. All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within twelve (12) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This change extends the period for disclosures from six to twelve months. This creates a contradiction if, for example, Clause 7 requires return of Confidential Information upon termination of the disclosure period. Now it's unclear whether it terminates after 6 or 12 months.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the disclosure period from 6 to 12 months. The contradicted text states that the recipient will return confidential information upon request, implying that the return is triggered by the termination of the disclosure period. Because the disclosure period has been extended, it is unclear whether the recipient is obligated to return the information after 6 months or 12 months, creating an ambiguity and inconsistency, and therefore a contradiction. The change alters the timing of a crucial event, creating incompatibility with the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business and trade secret information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes 'trade secret' from the definition of Confidential Information. Clause 6, prevents Recipient from disclosing 'Confidential Information.' Now, it's unclear whether trade secrets are protected under this clause, creating an ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'trade secret' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The contradicted text states that the Recipient will not disclose 'Confidential Information'. By removing 'trade secret' from the definition, it becomes unclear whether trade secrets are protected under the non-disclosure clause, creating an ambiguity and thus a contradiction as the contradicted text assumes 'trade secrets' are 'Confidential Information'.

perturbation.original_text: Where practical, Confidential Information shall be disclosed in Documentary or tangible form marked "Confidential". Where disclosures are made orally or by visual inspection, the Disclosor shall confirm in writing the fact and general nature of each disclosure within a reasonable time after each such disclosure is made.
perturbation.changed_text: Where practical, Confidential Information should be disclosed in Documentary or tangible form marked "Confidential". Where disclosures are made orally or by visual inspection, the Disclosor may confirm in writing the fact and general nature of each disclosure within a reasonable time after each such disclosure is made.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall' to 'should' and 'shall confirm' to 'may confirm' makes the confirmation of oral disclosures optional. However, if clause 2 relies on this written confirmation to define what's considered Confidential Information, it creates a contradiction. Now, it is optional if the Discloser confirms in writing, and may not be included as Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used herein, the term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed to include any technical, marketing, business information disclosed by the Disclosor, but excludes such information which: (a)is presently in Recipient's possession, provided that: (i) such information has not been obtained from the Disclosor; (ii) that such possession can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records and (iii) that Recipient so informs Disclosor in writing within 30 days from receipt of such Confidential Information; (b)is, or becomes, generally available to the public through, for example, such sources as patents or other generally circulated publications, and such availability to the public does not result from any fault of the Recipient; (c)is received by the Recipient in written form from a third party having no obligation to the Disclosor to keep it confidential; or (d)is independently developed by the Recipient provided that such development can be demonstrated by the Recipient's written records.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'should' and 'shall confirm' to 'may confirm' in the Changed Text makes the confirmation of oral disclosures optional. The Contradicted Text, defining 'Confidential Information,' implicitly relies on this written confirmation for information disclosed orally or visually to be included as Confidential Information. The changed text now allows for oral disclosures to not be confirmed in writing, and thus may not be included as Confidential Information, directly contradicting the definition in the Contradicted Text. This aligns with the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition, where inconsistent definitions create ambiguity and make enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that the sole purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take all reasonable steps necessary to hold in strictest confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information solely for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose shall be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees that the primary purpose for the Disclosor disclosing its Confidential Information to the Recipient is to enable Recipient to evaluate the Confidential Information for purposes of the Discussion. The Recipient will take reasonable steps necessary to hold in confidence all Confidential Information and to use such Confidential Information primarily for this purpose. Any use by the Recipient of such Confidential Information for any other purpose may be considered unauthorized.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'sole' to 'primary', 'strictest confidence' to 'confidence', and 'will take all reasonable steps' to 'will take reasonable steps' weakens the obligations around confidentiality. It introduces ambiguity about whether other uses are completely prohibited. Specifically, Clause 6 outrightly says Recipient will not disclose Confidential Information to others, and this pertubation, contradicts this prior statement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the sole purpose of the disclosure was to evaluate the confidential information and that the recipient would hold it in 'strictest confidence' and use it 'solely' for that purpose. The changed text weakens these obligations by changing 'sole' to 'primary,' 'strictest confidence' to 'confidence,' and 'will take all reasonable steps' to 'will take reasonable steps.' The contradicted text states that the recipient 'will not' disclose the confidential information to others. By weakening the confidentiality obligations, the changed text introduces ambiguity about whether other uses are permissible, which directly conflicts with the contradicted text's outright prohibition of disclosure or use for any other purpose.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information who are bound in writing to keep it confidential and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement that employees, agents, and representatives be 'bound in writing to keep it confidential'. This introduces a direct conflict with clause 6 which states Recipient will not disclose Confidential Information. Now, if employees aren't under NDA's, then Recipient could disclose through them and potentially contradict Clause 6.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient agrees that it will not use the Confidential, nor disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient, except with the prior written consent of Disclosor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement that employees, agents, and representatives be bound in writing to keep the confidential information confidential. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which states that the Recipient will not disclose the Confidential Information to others through any act or omission by Recipient. Without a written agreement binding the employees, agents, and representatives to confidentiality, the Recipient could disclose the information through them, violating the contradicted text. This fits the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' as essential language has been removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in another section.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient shall remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by Disclosor to Recipient may remain the property of Disclosor. The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall remain' to 'may remain' introduces ambiguity about the ownership of Confidential Information. Clause 8, which addresses intellectual property, could now conflict with Clause 7, given the ambiguity introduced on whether all the Confidential Information, shall still remain the property of Discloser.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Neither this Agreement nor the disclosure of any information by the Disclosor shall be deemed to constitute by implication or otherwise, a vesting of any title or interest or a grant of any license, immunity or other right to the Recipient with regard to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Confidential Information 'shall remain' the property of the Discloser, implying a definitive ownership. The changed text modifies this to 'may remain,' introducing ambiguity. The contradicted text states that no right to the Recipient with regard to the Confidential Information shall be constituted, but the change from shall to may directly breaks this compatibility because the contradicted section expects that all information disclosed will remain with the Discloser, but the modification allows that it might not. Since the definition of Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction, states that 'Ambiguities occur when key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently in different parts of a contract, making enforcement difficult or interpretation unclear.', then a clear contradiction exists.
